title: High-Quality BLS Inside ACLS & Team Dynamics
learningObjectives: State the compression rate, depth, and recoil targets that must be preserved throughout an ACLS resuscitation.
Explain chest compression fraction (CCF) and why it is a core team performance metric.
Describe the ventilation strategy once an advanced airway is placed (continuous compressions + 1 breath every 6 seconds).
List the core elements of effective resuscitation team dynamics.
Describe closed-loop communication and give an example.
Explain the concept of constructive intervention and when a team member should use it.
Describe the role of a dedicated CPR coach during a resuscitation.

summary.title: Why BLS Quality Doesn't Stop When ACLS Starts
summary.content: Every ACLS intervention is built on top of high-quality BLS. Adding drugs, airways, and rhythm therapy does not lower the bar for compressions — it raises the stakes of protecting them. Teams that let compression quality slip while focusing on advanced tasks lose the single strongest driver of survival.

summary.title: Compression Quality Targets in ACLS
summary.content: Rate 100–120/min; depth at least 2 in (5 cm) and not more than 2.4 in (6 cm); full chest recoil between compressions; minimize interruptions and keep any pause under 10 seconds; switch the compressor roughly every 2 minutes (or sooner if fatigued) to prevent quality decay. Chest compression fraction (CCF) — the proportion of resuscitation time spent actually compressing — should be at least 60%, and ideally above 80%; it is one of the most important team performance metrics because every pause, no matter how well-intentioned, drops it.

summary.title: Ventilation Once an Advanced Airway Is Placed
summary.content: Before an advanced airway, compressions and breaths remain cycled 30:2. Once a supraglottic airway or endotracheal tube is in place, compressions become continuous (no more pausing for breaths) and a single breath is delivered every 6 seconds (about 10 breaths/min), timed independently of the compression cycle. Avoid excessive ventilation rate or volume — over-ventilation raises intrathoracic pressure, reduces venous return, and lowers cardiac output during CPR.

summary.title: Core Elements of Team Dynamics
summary.content: Effective resuscitation teams rely on: clearly defined roles known to everyone before the code; closed-loop communication (order given by name and content, receiver repeats it back, sender confirms); clear, concise messages instead of vague or shouted ones; knowing and communicating your own limitations before attempting a task; constructive intervention when an error is about to happen; frequent reevaluation of the patient and plan; and mutual respect maintained even under stress.

summary.title: Closed-Loop Communication in Practice
summary.content: The team leader assigns a task to a named person ('Sarah, push epinephrine 1 mg now'), the receiver acknowledges and states the action ('Giving epinephrine 1 mg now'), and confirms completion ('Epinephrine 1 mg given'). This loop prevents duplicated or missed interventions and keeps the whole team's mental model synchronized, especially in a loud, high-stress environment.

summary.title: Constructive Intervention and the CPR Coach
summary.content: Any team member who notices an error — a drug about to be given twice, a pulse check taking too long, compression depth trailing off — should speak up immediately and respectfully; a good leader invites this rather than punishing it. Many systems now designate a CPR coach whose only job is to watch compression quality (rate, depth, recoil, pauses) and give real-time feedback, freeing the team leader to focus on rhythm interpretation and overall management.

clinicalPearls: CCF is a team score, not an individual one — everyone's pauses count against it.
Once an advanced airway is in, stop counting cycles of 30:2 — it's continuous compressions plus one breath every 6 seconds.
A closed loop takes two seconds and prevents a double dose or a missed task; skipping it to 'save time' usually costs more time later.
Silence during a resuscitation is a red flag — a quiet team is not necessarily a smooth one.
redFlags: Compression pauses creeping past 10 seconds for pulse checks, rhythm analysis, or airway attempts.
No one has been assigned to track time and compression quality (no CPR coach or timer role).
Team members hesitating to speak up about a perceived error because of hierarchy.
commonMistakes: Continuing 30:2 cycling after an advanced airway is already in place.
Vague, unaddressed orders ('someone give epi') instead of naming a specific person.
Allowing compression rate to drift above 120/min under adrenaline and stress, sacrificing depth and recoil.
memoryAids: 'Airway in, cycle out' — advanced airway means continuous compressions, no more 30:2.
Closed loop: Say it, do it, confirm it.
CCF ≥60%, ideally >80% — protect it like a vital sign.

decisionTrees.title: Ventilation Strategy During Arrest
decisionTrees.content:
Cardiac arrest, CPR ongoing
  → No advanced airway yet: 30 compressions : 2 breaths, minimize pause for breaths
  → Advanced airway placed (SGA or ETT) and confirmed
     → Continuous compressions (no pause for breaths)
     → 1 breath every 6 seconds (~10/min), avoid hyperventilation
  → Reassess quality every ~2 minutes: rate, depth, recoil, CCF, switch compressor

references: AHA 2025 Guidelines for CPR and ECC — Part 9: Adult Advanced Life Support (CPR Quality)
AHA 2025 Guidelines for CPR and ECC — Adult Basic Life Support (Ventilation With an Advanced Airway)
